scenarios.title: Admission reform within the current structure
scenarios.goal: Reduce the SES bias of entry into 6- and 8-year gimnazium tracks without changing the structure, by reforming the admission examination and admission rules.
mechanism: Replace the coachable central written admission examination with age-appropriate, less preparation-sensitive assessment plus school recommendation quotas [evidence: moderate]
SES-aware admission rules (weighted lottery above a threshold, or district quotas) reduce the composition skew of entrants [evidence: moderate]
Broader entry pools weaken the incentive for early private test-preparation [evidence: weak]
scenarios.evidence_status: moderate — Moderate: admission-rule effects on intake composition are well documented abroad; effects on system-level equity are indirect.
assumptions: Elite gimnaziums comply rather than route selection through informal channels.
A workable SES indicator exists at application time.
expected_benefits: More balanced intake into selective tracks within 2-3 admission cycles [evidence: moderate]
Reduced test-preparation arms race pressure on age-10 children [evidence: weak]
scenarios.equity_impact: Directly targets the entry gate, so gains accrue to high-ability low-SES pupils; does nothing for the majority who remain in general schools, and may relabel rather than reduce sorting if compliance is weak.
cost_categories: One-off: assessment redesign and piloting (low, central budget)
Recurring: administration of new admission process (low)
Political capital: moderate — concentrated on elite-school stakeholders
implementation_steps: Education ministry + assessment agency — design and pilot the new assessment in volunteer districts
Legislator — amend admission decree; define SES weighting
School district centres and maintainers — run first reformed admission cycle with monitoring
Independent evaluator — publish intake-composition evaluation; adjust rules
political_risks: Elite-school and middle-class parent opposition to lottery elements; framed as 'punishing talent'.
Church and private maintainers may claim exemption, hollowing out the rule.
uncertainties: Behavioural response of elite schools (informal selection channels). (confidence: low; would be reduced by: compliance audit design in the pilot)
Size of the intake-composition shift achievable by rule change alone. (confidence: medium; would be reduced by: pilot-district admission data after cycle 1)
Whether reduced coachability survives the test-prep industry's adaptation. (confidence: low; would be reduced by: repeat coachability studies after 2 cycles)

scenarios.title: Gradual phase-down of 6/8-year entry places
scenarios.goal: Shrink early-selective intake over a decade by capping and gradually reducing entry places, while investing the freed capacity in strong programmes inside general schools.
mechanism: Annual entry-place caps reduce the share of a cohort selected at 10/12, mechanically lowering early sorting [evidence: strong]
Advanced-programme funding in general schools retains ambitious families in the common track [evidence: weak]
A published decade-long trajectory lets schools and families adapt without cliff effects [evidence: moderate]
scenarios.evidence_status: moderate — Moderate: the sorting reduction is mechanical; the retention of demand in general schools is the unevidenced link.
assumptions: General schools can absorb and challenge high-achievers if funded for it.
Caps are enforceable across state, church and private maintainers.
expected_benefits: Steadily falling early-sorting share of each cohort (measurable annually) [evidence: strong]
Stronger peer composition in urban general-school upper grades [evidence: moderate]
scenarios.equity_impact: Benefits the non-selected majority through improved peer composition and reinvested capacity; the residual selective places become scarcer and, without S1-type admission reform, even more SES-skewed.
cost_categories: Recurring: advanced-programme funding in general schools (medium, formula-based)
Political capital: high and sustained over a decade
implementation_steps: Legislator — enact the cap trajectory with felmenő rendszer guarantees for enrolled pupils
Education ministry — launch the general-school advanced-programme fund
Maintainers (state, church, private) — apply annual caps; report intake composition
Independent evaluator — mid-course evaluation at year 5 with a pre-committed adjustment rule
political_risks: A successor government can freeze or reverse the trajectory cheaply (Polish precedent).
Scarcer selective places intensify the admission arms race in the transition years.
Church maintainers may litigate or negotiate exemptions.
uncertainties: Whether general-school programmes actually retain high-achieving families. (confidence: low; would be reduced by: tracked cohort study in early-cap districts)
Reversal risk after a change of government. (confidence: low; would be reduced by: cross-party pact or statutory entrenchment attempts)
Peer-effect size on remaining general-school classes. (confidence: medium; would be reduced by: NABC panel analysis as caps bite)

scenarios.title: Comprehensive school to age 14 (structural reform)
scenarios.goal: End between-school academic selection before age 14 by phasing out 6- and 8-year gimnazium entry, moving first selection to the end of grade 8, with differentiated teaching inside a common school.
mechanism: Later between-school selection weakens the SES-performance link, per cross-country tracking evidence [evidence: strong]
Common-school peer composition raises outcomes of disadvantaged pupils more than it lowers those of advantaged ones (asymmetric peer effects) [evidence: contested]
In-school differentiation (setting, enrichment) substitutes for between-school tracks if teachers are trained for it [evidence: moderate]
scenarios.evidence_status: contested — Contested overall: the strongest structural evidence supports it, but peer-effect asymmetry and Hungarian implementation capacity are genuinely disputed.
assumptions: Teacher capacity for differentiated instruction can be built at national scale within the transition window.
Sorting does not simply migrate to school choice between general schools and to non-state maintainers.
Political ownership survives at least two electoral cycles.
expected_benefits: Weaker SES-outcome gradient within a decade of full phase-in (Polish/Finnish direction of effect) [evidence: moderate]
Later, better-informed track choice at 14 reduces misallocation of talent [evidence: moderate]
scenarios.equity_impact: The largest expected equity gain of all scenarios — and the largest risk: if middle-class families exit to non-state schools, segregation could worsen relative to baseline.
cost_categories: One-off: nationwide teacher retraining for differentiation (high)
One-off: network and building reconfiguration, bundled with demographic consolidation (high)
Political capital: very high, sustained for a decade
implementation_steps: Government + opposition (pact attempt) — negotiate a cross-party framework before legislation
Education ministry + teacher-education universities — launch differentiation retraining at scale; pilot comprehensive model in 2-3 districts
Legislator — amend the public education act: stop new 6/8-year entry from a named year, felmenő rendszer for enrolled pupils
Maintainers + district centres — execute the phase-out with town-level network plans
political_risks: Highest-intensity opposition of all scenarios: mobilised parents, elite schools, parts of the teaching profession.
Polish-style reversal after government change destroys sunk transition costs.
Non-state maintainer exemption fights could produce a two-tier system worse than baseline.
uncertainties: Scale of middle-class exit to non-state schools. (confidence: low; would be reduced by: maintainer-linked enrolment monitoring in pilot districts)
Whether in-school differentiation reaches acceptable quality at national scale. (confidence: low; would be reduced by: pilot-district classroom observation + NABC results)
Net peer effect on previously selected high-achievers. (confidence: medium; would be reduced by: quasi-experimental analysis of cap-induced variation from S2-type policies)

scenarios.title: Keep the structure, compensate the general schools
scenarios.goal: Accept early selection as politically fixed and offset its equity costs by a Portuguese-style package: targeted funding, teacher incentives and support programmes for general schools serving disadvantaged pupils.
mechanism: Targeted resources and retention reduction improved outcomes in Portugal without changing selection age [evidence: moderate]
Salary premia shift experienced teachers toward disadvantaged schools, raising instruction quality where sorting hurts most [evidence: moderate]
Compensation is additive: it does not touch the sorting mechanism itself [evidence: strong]
scenarios.evidence_status: moderate — Moderate: the package's components are individually evidenced; whether they can offset selection as early as Hungary's is the open question.
assumptions: Compensation can meaningfully offset, not just mask, the effects of early sorting.
Targeting formulas reach the intended schools without stigmatising them.
expected_benefits: Improved outcomes in disadvantaged general schools within 5 years (Portuguese trajectory) [evidence: moderate]
Politically inexpensive; implementable under any government [evidence: strong]
scenarios.equity_impact: Helps the disadvantaged majority where they actually are; leaves the sorting mechanism and its signalling effects intact, so the SES gradient narrows at best partially.
cost_categories: Recurring: targeted school funding formula (high — this is the package)
Recurring: teacher premia in disadvantaged schools (medium-high)
implementation_steps: Education ministry + finance ministry — define the targeting formula (SES-index based) and budget line
District centres — contract participating schools with improvement plans (TEIP-style)
Teacher policy unit — introduce premia and mentoring pipelines for disadvantaged schools
Independent evaluator — evaluate against pre-registered outcome targets; sunset or scale
political_risks: Budget vulnerability: compensation lines are the first cut in fiscal consolidation.
Risk of legitimising early selection permanently ('we already compensate').
uncertainties: Whether compensation can offset selection effects this early; no country has demonstrated it at Hungary's selection age. (confidence: low; would be reduced by: pre-registered evaluation of the pilot against matched districts)
Teacher premium size needed to actually move experienced teachers. (confidence: medium; would be reduced by: discrete-choice study on teacher mobility)
Durability of the budget line across fiscal cycles. (confidence: low; would be reduced by: statutory earmarking attempt + historical budget analysis)
